message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 12
Recent Green phase: No code change — "2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G" passed immediately, confirming different component types are grouped separately (no cross-type block).

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. No code changed this cycle; document if nothing is warranted.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the current implementation and test files to evaluate refactoring opportunities.
Let me verify the current test state before evaluating.

content.text:
All 12 tests pass. Now let me evaluate the implementation following the refactoring process.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Reviewing each function name against its actual purpose:

- `componentBase(count)` — computes base premium for a count of alike components, applying the block rule. Name reveals intent. **Keep.**
- `baseForType(type, count)` — computes base premium for `count` items of a given `type`, routing components vs. main items. Clear. **Keep.**
- `countByType(items)` — groups items into a `Map` of type→count. Clear. **Keep.**
- `baseForItems(items)` — sums the base premium across all item types. Clear. **Keep.**
- `priceQuote(items)` — produces a `{ premium }` quote from items. Clear. **Keep.**
- `runScenario(scenario)` — runs each step and collects results. Clear. **Keep.**

Constants (`PROCESSING_FEE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`, `COMPONENT_BASE`, `BLOCK_SIZE`, `BLOCK_BASE`, `BASE_PREMIUMS`, `COMPONENT_TYPES`) are all well-named and domain-aligned. No naming improvement warranted.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

I'll measure the body of `priceQuote` (the locus of the just-confirmed behavior and the most likely candidate) and survey the whole file.

Surveying the file for refactoring candidates against the Four Rules:

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 12 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The pipeline `countByType → baseForType → baseForItems` reads cleanly. Component vs. main-item logic is explicit. No clarity deficit.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** Scanning for duplication — `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type)` branch and the `BASE_PREMIUMS[type] ?? 0` lookup are each represented once. The reduce-into-Map idiom appears once. No duplication exists.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Each function carries distinct responsibility. The decomposition (`componentBase`, `baseForType`, `countByType`, `baseForItems`) maps directly to the kata's distinct rules (block pricing, type routing, grouping, summation). Collapsing any of these would merge separable concerns and reduce clarity — a Rule 2 regression for no Rule 4 benefit, since the element count is already minimal per responsibility.

## Step 3: APP Mass of `priceQuote` body

```
const policyBase = baseForItems(items);                          // assignment 6 + invocation 2 + binding(items) 1 = 9
const firstInsuranceSurcharge = policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE; // assignment 6 + binding policyBase 1 + binding RATE 1 + op* (invocation) 2 = 10
const premium = Math.ceil(policyBase + ... + PROCESSING_FEE);     // assignment 6 + Math.ceil 2 + 2× '+' 4 + bindings(policyBase,surcharge,FEE) 3 = 15
return { premium };                                               // binding 1
```

Total ≈ 35. The `firstInsuranceSurcharge` explaining variable adds an assignment (mass 6) but materially improves intent — this is a deliberate **Rule 2 over APP** tradeoff. Inlining it would drop mass but obscure the 10% first-insurance concept that the tests assert ("+ first-insurance 10%"). Not warranted.

## Step 4–6: Refactoring Decision

I attempted to identify at least one improvement across all four rules and APP. No change improves the code without regressing a higher-priority rule:

**Refactoring Evaluation — no change applied this cycle:**

- Naming: every function and constant already reveals intent; no rename improves clarity.
- Rule 2 (Intent): the `countByType → baseForType → baseForItems` pipeline and the explaining variable `firstInsuranceSurcharge` are already maximally clear; any APP-motivated inlining would obscure domain concepts the tests assert.
- Rule 3 (Duplication): no obvious or conceptual duplication exists — each rule (block pricing, type routing, grouping, summation, surcharge) has a single representation.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): the four helpers each correspond to a distinct kata rule; collapsing them would merge separable concerns and regress Rule 2 for no net benefit.
- APP: the only mass reduction available (inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, ~−6 mass) is rejected because Rule 2 trumps APP.

This cycle ("2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G") confirmed existing grouping behavior with **no code change**, so the structure was already exercised and clean before this cycle. The implementation is in an already-refactored state.

## Step 7: Report

```
Refactoring Complete:
Refactoring: none warranted this cycle — see evaluation below
Mass Change: n/a (no change applied)
Tests: 12 passing
```

Refactoring outcome for this cycle: **no change applied**, by design.

- Files reviewed: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_05-27-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` and `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_05-27-45_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`
- Tests: 12 passing, 29 todo — confirmed green before and after evaluation.
- The Green phase added no code (the test passed against existing grouping logic), so there was no fresh complexity to clean up.

Why no refactoring was applied, by rule priority:
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): all 12 green.
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): all names (`countByType`, `baseForType`, `baseForItems`, `componentBase`, `priceQuote`, `runScenario`) and constants already reveal intent. The explaining variable `firstInsuranceSurcharge` clarifies the 10% rule the tests assert.
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): each domain rule has a single representation; no obvious or conceptual duplication.
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): the four helpers each map to a distinct kata rule; merging them would regress Rule 2 with no benefit.
- APP: the only available mass reduction (inlining `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, roughly −6 mass) is rejected because Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP.

The code is already in a clean, refactored state. Proceed to the next Red phase.
